title: What Will Happen to Iran’s Sea Mines?
gist: Iran's cheap sea mines choked ~20% of world oil through the Strait of Hormuz, exposing US and Chinese dependence on open seas and the absence of any nonproliferation regime for mines—with China both a supplier and a vulnerable importer.

insights.claim: Starting late February, Iran effectively blocked 20 percent of the world's oil—and nearly half of China's oil supply—for months, stranding around 2,000 ships in the Persian Gulf.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Quantifies a chokepoint shock that reprices oil, tankers, and shipping insurance globally.

insights.claim: The Hormuz crisis pushed US gas prices above $4 per gallon and drove Chinese crude imports to their lowest level in a decade, forcing Chinese firms to draw down oil reserves.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Direct read-through to energy prices and Chinese strategic reserve drawdowns during chokepoint disruptions.

insights.claim: Mere uncertainty about whether mines are present—regardless of actual mining—has been enough to significantly slow ship movement through the strait.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Perceived risk alone reprices shipping—insurers and charterers act on belief, not confirmation.
insights.quote: whether there are mines there or not, people think there’s mines there and they will operate accordingly

insights.claim: According to US officials cited by the NYT, Iran cannot locate all the mines it laid and lacks the capability to remove them, hindering efforts to reopen the strait.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Clearance uncertainty could keep Hormuz risk premia elevated for an extended period.

insights.claim: Sea mines are cheap and simple yet have no nonproliferation regime—the Ottawa Convention and Wassenaar Arrangement do not effectively control them, unlike missiles, nuclear material, or chemical weapons.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: An overlooked, unregulated weapons category poses systemic chokepoint risk—a policy gap that could persist.

insights.claim: By the 2000s Iran's arsenal included naval mines ten times as powerful as those used in the 1980s tanker wars.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Mine lethality has scaled sharply, raising the damage potential of any future chokepoint closure.

insights.claim: China may have produced some of Iran's sea mines—having sold the EM-52 rising mine per a 1994 report—meaning Chinese-made weapons may have helped choke China's own oil supply.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Illustrates supplier blowback risk; China's arms exports can boomerang against its own trade interests.

insights.claim: China has a large, growing naval mine capability plus mine-laying drones that could hinder US operations in a Taiwan scenario, and as of February was in late-stage talks to sell supersonic anti-ship missiles to Iran.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Chinese sea-denial capability is a direct factor in Taiwan-contingency risk assessments.

insights.claim: China operates the world's largest navy but has relatively weak mine countermeasures and imports more oil through Hormuz than any other country, leaving it acutely vulnerable to sea mines.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: China's chokepoint dependence is a strategic weakness that could shift its stance on mine control.

insights.claim: The Hormuz crisis may make Beijing receptive to stronger sea mine regulation despite its role as a producer.
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: A potential opening for arms-control cooperation that would reduce future chokepoint risk.

insights.claim: The author urges a sea mine technology control regime modeled on the 1987 Missile Technology Control Regime, ideally including China, plus US ratification of UNCLOS (which neither the US nor Iran has joined).
insights.speaker: Josh Waldman
insights.so_what: Signals the regulatory direction of travel; absence of such a regime keeps chokepoint tail-risk structurally high.
